structured_choices.data_source: 1,000 labeled CT scan images from a two-year collection period covering patients with suspected lung cancer. Source: clinical imaging archive with documented acquisition protocols and radiologist annotation logs. Images span multiple scanner models, reconstruction parameters, and institutional sites to reflect real-world variability.
structured_choices.label_definition: Ground truth label is established through consensus adjudication: nodule presence/absence is determined by agreement between at least two independent senior radiologists (≥10 years experience). Disagreements are resolved by a third expert radiologist blind to model predictions. This reduces individual interpreter bias and establishes a more reliable target than single-reader annotation.
structured_choices.split_method: Stratified random split (not temporal, as no real deployment sequence exists; not by radiologist, as that would confound model performance with reader identity). Split stratified on: (1) nodule class (positive/negative), (2) image quality tier (high/medium/low based on pre-study quality audit), and (3) acquisition site (if multi-site data). Allocation: 60% train (600 images), 20% validation (200 images), 20% held-out test (200 images). Stratification ensures each fold preserves the true class distribution and quality/site variation, preventing validation/test sets from being artificially easy or hard.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (intensity normalization, histogram equalization, artifact masking) are fit exclusively on the training set (600 images). The fitted statistics and parameters are then applied to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents information leakage from validation/test images into the preprocessing pipeline, which would artificially inflate reported performance.
structured_choices.features: Raw input features are the pixel-level intensities from CT scans (Hounsfield units), preprocessed as follows: (1) Intensity normalization to zero mean and unit variance using statistics from training set only; (2) Standardized windowing to [−1024, +400] HU to emphasize lung tissue; (3) Resizing to fixed 512×512 resolution with bilinear interpolation; (4) No hand-crafted radiomics features—the CNN learns hierarchical representations directly from pixel data, which is appropriate for end-to-end learning. No features encode the label or future information; all inputs are pre-diagnosis imaging.
structured_choices.model: A ResNet-50 architecture pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the training set. ResNet-50 is chosen because: (a) it has demonstrated strong performance on medical imaging tasks with limited data when initialized from pre-trained weights; (b) it provides a modern, reproducible baseline; (c) skip connections mitigate vanishing gradient problems in deeper networks. Hyperparameters (learning rate, batch size, regularization) are tuned on the validation set using grid search or Bayesian optimization.
structured_choices.baseline: The baseline is the consensus radiologist assessment (the ground truth label itself, aggregated performance from the two-radiologist consensus adjudication process). To make this a fair comparison: (1) baseline performance is evaluated using sensitivity and specificity on the same held-out test set as the CNN; (2) radiologists are blinded to CNN predictions to prevent confirmation bias; (3) we measure the agreement rate and disagreement patterns between individual radiologists and the consensus standard on the test set to characterize baseline variability. The hypothesis claim is that CNN sensitivity > consensus radiologist sensitivity AND CNN specificity > consensus radiologist specificity (not just that the CNN is non-trivially different).
structured_choices.primary_metric: Sensitivity (recall for positive class: TP / (TP + FN)), justified as follows: In lung nodule detection, missing a true nodule (false negative) has severe clinical consequences—potential delay in cancer diagnosis and treatment. Sensitivity directly quantifies the model's ability to avoid this failure mode. For early cancer detection, high sensitivity is often prioritized over specificity because the cost of a missed diagnosis outweighs the cost of further investigation on a false positive. Secondary evaluation will include specificity to ensure false positives do not become burdensome.
secondary_metrics: Specificity (true negative rate: TN / (TN + FP)) — ensures the model does not over-flag benign findings
ROC-AUC — summarizes performance across all decision thresholds and is insensitive to class imbalance
Precision-Recall AUC — emphasizes positive class performance when class imbalance is severe
F1-score at optimal threshold — balances precision and recall if a single operating point is needed
Calibration plot and expected calibration error (ECE) — verifies that predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for clinical decision-making
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed using the validation set (200 images) only. The test set is never used during model selection. Grid search or Bayesian optimization explores: learning rate {1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2}, batch size {16, 32, 64}, L2 regularization {1e-5, 1e-4, 1e-3}, dropout {0.3, 0.5}. The model checkpoint with the highest validation sensitivity is selected (or if multiple have similar sensitivity, the one with highest specificity to avoid degenerate solutions). This discipline prevents overfitting to the validation set.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The 200-image test set is locked and not examined until the final experiment run. Model selection, architecture choice, and hyperparameter tuning are all concluded before opening the test set. Final performance metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, calibration) are computed once on the test set and reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrapping with 1,000 resamples). The test set is used exactly once; no retuning or post-hoc adjustments are permitted after test evaluation.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Radiologist experience is controlled by: (1) restricting consensus adjudication to senior radiologists (≥10 years lung imaging experience), ensuring baseline expertise is high; (2) stratifying the data split by acquisition site and image quality so that both model and radiologist assessments are evaluated on the same distribution; (3) on the test set, comparing individual radiologist performance (as single readers, not consensus) to model performance, allowing direct comparison of a single CNN vs. a single radiologist; (4) reporting disagreement rates between radiologists and model on test images to qualitatively identify whether mismatches follow systematic patterns (e.g., model biased toward certain nodule sizes or locations). The experiment establishes: whether a CNN can match or exceed senior radiologist sensitivity/specificity on this dataset. It does not establish: generalization to future patients outside this institutional cohort, robustness to scanner model shifts, or suitability without radiologist oversight in clinical workflow.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Radiologist Assessment for Lung Nodule Detection

### 1. Data and Label Definition

The experiment uses 1,000 CT scan images collected over two years from patients with suspected lung cancer. To establish reliable ground truth and reduce individual interpreter bias, nodule presence/absence is determined by **consensus adjudication**: agreement between at least two independent senior radiologists (≥10 years lung imaging experience), with a third expert resolving any disagreements. Each radiologist independently reviews images blinded to other assessments. This consensus standard becomes the target label for model training and the baseline for comparison—it is more robust than any single radiologist's judgment.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

Images are divided using **stratified random allocation** (60% train, 20% validation, 20% test) stratified on three dimensions:
- **Nodule class**: ensures positive and negative cases are represented proportionally in each fold
- **Image quality tier**: high/medium/low based on pre-study quality audit, ensuring the model is evaluated on realistic variation
- **Acquisition site**: if multi-site data, prevents site-specific biases from concentrating in any one fold

This stratification is chosen over temporal or radiologist-based splits because: (a) no real deployment sequence exists, so temporal splits are artificial; (b) splitting by radiologist identity would confound model performance with reader effects rather than isolate model capability. Stratified random split ensures validation and test sets are representative of training distribution, preventing spurious performance inflation.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Construction

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only and applied to validation/test sets without refitting. This prevents information leakage. Preprocessing steps include:
- Intensity normalization (zero mean, unit variance using training set statistics)
- Standardized windowing to [−1024, +400] Hounsfield units to emphasize lung tissue
- Resizing to 512×512 with bilinear interpolation

No hand-crafted radiomics features are engineered; the CNN learns representations directly from pixel intensities, which is appropriate for end-to-end learning and avoids introducing domain bias.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The model is **ResNet-50 pre-trained on ImageNet, fine-tuned on the 600-image training set**. ResNet-50 is chosen for: robustness to vanishing gradients, strong empirical performance on medical imaging with limited data, and reproducibility.

The baseline is the **consensus radiologist assessment**, evaluated on the same held-out test set. This is a fair comparison because: (a) both CNN and radiologists assess identical test images; (b) radiologists are blind to model predictions to prevent bias; (c) both are measured using the same metrics. Additionally, individual radiologist performance (not consensus) is reported on the test set for direct 1-v-1 comparison with the model.

### 5. Metrics and Evaluation

**Primary metric: Sensitivity (TP / (TP + FN))**

Justification: Missed lung nodules have severe clinical consequences—delayed cancer diagnosis and treatment. Sensitivity quantifies the model's ability to avoid false negatives, which is the highest-priority failure mode in early detection. In lung cancer screening, sensitivity is typically prioritized over specificity.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **Specificity**: ensures false positives do not create clinical burden
- **ROC-AUC & Precision-Recall AUC**: provide threshold-agnostic performance summaries
- **Calibration plot & ECE**: verify predicted probabilities are well-calibrated for clinical decision-making
- **F1-score**: balances precision and recall at an optimal threshold if a single operating point is needed

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, batch size, L2 regularization, dropout) is performed on the 200-image **validation set only**, using grid search or Bayesian optimization. The model checkpoint with the highest validation sensitivity is selected. Crucially, the test set is never touched during tuning.

### 7. Test Set and Final Reporting

The 200-image test set is locked until model selection is complete. Final metrics (sensitivity, specificity, AUC, calibration) are computed once on the test set. 95% confidence intervals are estimated via bootstrap resampling (1,000 resamples). No retuning or adjustments are permitted after test evaluation.

### 8. Controlling for Confounds

Radiologist experience is controlled by restricting consensus adjudication to senior radiologists (≥10 years). Radiologist variability is characterized by: (a) reporting disagreement rates between individual radiologists and the model on test images; (b) qualitatively analyzing failure modes to identify systematic biases (e.g., model vs. radiologist sensitivity to nodule size or location); (c) comparing model performance to individual radiologist performance on the test set.

### 9. Scope of Claims

The experiment establishes whether a CNN can match or exceed senior radiologist sensitivity and specificity on this dataset. It **does not establish**: generalization to future institutional cohorts, robustness to scanner model shifts, or readiness for unilateral deployment without radiologist oversight. Deployment decisions require prospective validation on held-out populations and operational impact studies.
